ONE BURR BRIDGE REPORT


never MENTIONED NOW

pages.raw_text:
and that 1t has marked advan-
tages over the Monroe street
bridge" because of shorter CEN
ter span and owing to the greater
height of the river bed, thus re-
quiring less masonry. In conclud-
ing this report, Professor Burr
says:


The cost of such an alternative
structure, designed with the same
capacity roadway and sidewalks as
the Monroe street bridge, would
probably not much, If any, exceed
six-tenths of the estimated cost Of
either of the new structures COW
cred in my report to you Of Octo-
Der II of the current year. My
general recommendation in that
respect would not be changed, ex-
cept that the decreased cost Of a
REINFORCED concrete bridge on this
location would make that type of
structure all the more desirable"


In forwarding the letter to the
city council on October 10, 1907,
Mayor Moore wrote:


1T needs no comment of mine
further than that l am heartily in
favor of it,


The letter from Burr was read at
the meeting of the city council of
October %, 1907, and referred to
the committee of the whole, where
it has slept undisturbed until
brought to light this week.


Professor Burr's estimate of the
cost of the Monroe street bridge,
built of concrete, was $325,000, and
of steel $250,OOO. This left a fill
at either end that would cost, it is
estimated, $4,000, making the
grand total probably about 100000
for concrete and $325,O00 for steel.
On this showing concrete bridge
on the Lincoln street site would
cost only $4,000. a saving to the
taxpayers of $205,000, as there
would be no fill scarcely to make
on the Lincoln street site. A steel
bridge could be built there under
Burr's estimate for $150.000.


City Engineer Ralston's Monroe
street bridge will be only about
one-half the length of Professor
Burr's bridge, but the estimated
cost is $4,000. 1t is said that this
bridge will cost when the long fills
are made 100000, so the saving on
a bridge on the Lincoln street site
over a bridge to be built on the
Monroe street as planned by Engi-
neer Ralston would be even great
er than the bridge planned by Pro-
fessor Burr.
